planting.note: Direct sow in spring into lean, sandy ground.

directions: Sow thinly on poor, sandy soil where drainage is excellent and competition is low. Finely fringed, snowy-white flowers with a green eye release a sweet scent in the evening. Trim spent stems and let the tidy grassy clumps spread slowly at the front of a dry bed.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for bloom the same or following year.

directions: Sow shallowly and grow on in cool, bright conditions before planting into drained soil. This is the only truly yellow pink, carrying its small clustered flowers on wiry stems in summer. Deadhead to prolong the display and divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring into open, well-drained ground.

directions: Sow just under the surface into lean soil where plants will not be crowded out. Tall, narrow stems hold tight clusters of vivid magenta flowers well above grassy foliage, drawing butterflies. Leave some seed heads to ripen so it can naturalize through a meadow or gravel planting.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; often behaves as an annual or short-lived biennial.

directions: Scatter the seed on lean, dry soil and press in lightly for good contact. Slim upright stems carry small deep-pink flowers with pale speckles in mid to late summer. Allow a few plants to set seed if you want it to reappear in a wildflower or gravel setting.

planting.note: Sow in a cold frame in late winter for the chill it needs.

directions: Surface-sow onto gritty compost and expose the pots to cold to trigger even germination. Low tufts of narrow leaves produce large pink flowers with a buff reverse and a darker central zone. Give it very sharp drainage in a trough or rock garden and protect the crown from standing wet.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; often blooms the first year from an early sowing.

directions: Sow shallowly and grow on cool and bright before setting out into drained soil. Slender stems carry soft lilac to lavender flowers over a long summer season. Deadhead often to keep the display fresh and let a little seed drop for replacement plants.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or sow in a cold frame.

directions: Surface-sow onto gritty mix and keep just moist until the fine tufts appear. Clean single pink flowers with smooth-edged petals sit on short stems above grassy cushions. Ideal for a sunny rock crevice or wall, where it enjoys the sharp drainage and reflected warmth.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring, as named forms are grown vegetatively.

directions: Set young plants into gritty, alkaline soil with the crown just at the surface. This vigorous garden hybrid repeat-blooms with fragrant flowers over blue-grey mounds all summer. Shear lightly after each flush and take summer cuttings to keep a fresh stock coming along.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring into sharply drained soil.

directions: Set plants with the crown at soil level in gritty, alkaline ground to prevent rot. The steel-blue mat throws up masses of intensely fragrant magenta flowers in late spring. Shear the whole cushion after bloom to keep it tight and to encourage scattered repeat flowers.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; tolerates heat and humidity better than most pinks.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil and keep mulch away from the blue-grey crown. Soft pink, fringed flowers cover the spreading mat in mid to late spring with a clove scent. Trim back the flowering stems afterward to keep the ground-hugging foliage neat through summer.

planting.note: Plant small divisions in spring at the front of a bed or in a trough.

directions: Tuck plants into gritty soil where the diminutive mat can spread without competition. Tiny double ruby-pink flowers stud the dense blue-green cushion in late spring. Shear after flowering and enjoy its neat evergreen carpet through the rest of the year.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring; this heirloom is grown vegetatively.

directions: Set plants into drained, limey soil with the crown kept high and dry. This old cottage favorite bears fat, shaggy double white flowers with an intense clove perfume in early summer. Deadhead and trim after bloom, and root a few pipings each year to renew the short-lived clumps.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into free-draining soil.

directions: Plant with the crown at the surface in gritty, alkaline ground and full sun. This reliable Allwoodii hybrid produces fragrant salmon-pink double flowers with a darker eye over a very long season. Deadhead often and give a light shear midway to keep repeat flushes coming into autumn.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring; classic laced border pink.

directions: Set into sharply drained soil with the crown proud of the surface to avoid rot. Fragrant white double flowers are laced with a distinct raspberry-purple ring and margin. Remove faded blooms and trim after the first flush to keep the plant flowering repeatedly.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into drained, sunny ground.

directions: Plant into gritty, alkaline soil in full sun with good air movement. Double flowers in deep rose are boldly laced with a chocolate-maroon eye and edging, and carry a spicy scent. Deadhead through summer and shear lightly to encourage a fresh round of buds.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for possible first-year bloom, or sow in early summer.

directions: Barely cover the seed and grow on until sturdy before setting out. Very dark maroon-black flowers open above bronze-tinted foliage and are wonderfully scented and long-lasting when cut. Deadhead to extend bloom and let a few heads seed to keep this short-lived plant going.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring, as this novelty sets no viable seed.

directions: Set young plants into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. Instead of petals, each stem holds a fuzzy emerald-green pompon that looks like moss and lasts for weeks. Cut the long-stemmed, unusual heads for arrangements and shear plants back to prompt more.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; heat- and humidity-tolerant selection.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil in full sun where it can take summer heat. Bright rosy-pink single flowers cover the grassy mounds from late spring well into autumn. Shear off spent flowers to keep the long bloom season running and tidy the clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into sharply drained soil.

directions: Set plants with the crown at soil level in gritty, sunny ground. Large semi-double flowers in swirled pink and white sit on compact blue-grey mounds with strong fragrance. Deadhead and shear after the main flush to bring on repeat bloom into late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; bred for heat tolerance in southern gardens.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil in full sun with room for the mound to fill in. Large peachy-pink flowers are marked with a bold cherry-red eye and carry a light fragrance. Remove spent blooms to keep flushes coming from late spring through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into free-draining, sunny soil.

directions: Set plants at soil level in gritty ground and keep the crown dry. Frilly double flowers combine deep burgundy-red centers with creamy white edges and a spicy scent. Deadhead regularly and shear after the first big flush to prompt more bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant with the crown just at the surface in gritty, alkaline soil and full sun. Double flowers swirl raspberry-pink over a paler ground and give off a sweet clove fragrance. Trim off faded blooms and give a light shear to keep the compact mound flowering.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; part of the Fruit Punch series.

directions: Set plants into gritty, well-drained soil with the crown at the surface. Bright cranberry-red flowers rise on short stems above compact blue-green mounds with a clove scent. Deadhead through the season and shear after the main flush for strong repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into sunny, drained soil.

directions: Plant at soil level in gritty ground and keep the crown free of wet mulch. Fragrant cherry-red flowers cover tidy blue-green cushions from late spring onward. Remove faded flowers and shear the mound lightly to keep it blooming and neat.

planting.note: Plant small divisions in spring in a rock garden or trough.

directions: Tuck plants into very gritty, alkaline soil where drainage is excellent. Double soft-pink flowers with a slightly darker center sit just above tight silvery cushions. Shear after bloom and avoid winter wet at the crown for a long-lived alpine pink.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into sharply drained soil.

directions: Set into gritty soil with the crown at the surface in a sunny rock garden. Semi-double pink flowers with a maroon eye are strongly clove-scented above compact grey cushions. Trim spent stems after flowering to keep the little mound dense and healthy.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into free-draining, sunny soil.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in gritty ground and full sun. Vivid magenta-pink single flowers glow above compact deep-green mounds and repeat well. Shear the whole plant after the first flush to bring on another wave of bright bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Set plants into sharply drained soil with the crown kept high and dry. Double fiery scarlet-red flowers stand out above frosty blue-grey foliage in late spring. Deadhead and shear lightly afterward to encourage repeat flowering through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into sunny, drained soil.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil in full sun with good air circulation. Fragrant flowers open near-white and deepen through pink to rose, so several shades appear on the plant at once. Deadhead frequently to keep the changing color show going all summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring; historic laced pink.

directions: Set plants into gritty, alkaline soil with the crown proud of the surface. Semi-double white flowers are edged and laced with deep chocolate-maroon and are lightly scented. Deadhead after the main flush and root cuttings to keep this old variety going.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into free-draining soil.

directions: Plant with the crown at the surface in gritty, sunny ground. Double flowers in soft buttery cream flushed with pink carry a warm clove fragrance over blue-grey mounds. Deadhead and lightly shear to keep the repeat-blooming plant tidy and productive.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into sharply drained, sunny soil.

directions: Set plants at soil level in gritty ground and keep the crown dry. Brilliant magenta single flowers smother the low silvery-blue mat over a long season. Shear off spent blooms to keep the compact carpet flowering repeatedly into autumn.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring; tall interspecific hybrid for cutting.

directions: Plant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun and give tall stems some support. Dense heads of rich red flowers rise on long, strong stems that are excellent for cutting. Harvest stems as the heads open and remove spent flowers to lengthen the bloom season.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; this interspecific hybrid blooms the first year.

directions: Barely cover the seed, keep warm, and grow on before setting into fertile soil. Large domed heads mixing rose and purple shades top tall stems that make long-lasting cut flowers. Cut freely and deadhead to keep the long summer bloom season running.

planting.note: Set divisions or potted plants out in early spring as new growth emerges.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown just below the soil surface in humus-rich, moist ground with morning sun and afternoon shade. Keep evenly watered through spring; the plant naturally yellows and goes dormant by midsummer, so pair it with later-emerging perennials. Arching stems hung with pink, heart-shaped lockets appear from mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a shaded, sheltered bed.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in cool, rich, well-drained ground and mulch to keep roots moist. Shelter the brittle stems from wind and avoid letting the soil dry during bloom. Pure white lockets dangle along the branches in spring before the foliage fades in summer heat.

planting.note: Plant in spring where bright foliage lights up a shady corner.

directions: Give this selection dappled light to protect its chartreuse-gold leaves from scorch while keeping the color bright. Plant in rich, moisture-retentive soil and water during dry spells. Rose-pink hearts hang against the golden foliage from spring into early summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in part shade for the deepest flower color.

directions: Set plants into cool, rich soil where dark burgundy stems and red-hued new growth show off against a shaded backdrop. Keep the ground evenly moist during active growth and mulch to hold moisture. Cherry-red lockets tipped in white appear in spring, and the foliage recedes as summer heats up.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to prevent leaf scorch.

directions: Combine the glowing gold leaves and pure white flowers by planting in humus-rich, consistently moist soil with protection from hot afternoon sun. Water during dry weather to keep the foliage from crisping. Arching sprays of white hearts open in spring above the bright leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in dappled shade with moist, rich soil.

directions: Grow this white-flowered form in cool, moisture-retentive woodland soil and shear lightly after the first flush to encourage repeat bloom. The finely cut gray-green foliage stays attractive through the season with regular water. Creamy white flowers rise on slender stems from late spring into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded border or woodland edge.

directions: Set this white selection in fertile, evenly moist soil in part to full shade for a long season of bloom. Deadhead spent stems and keep the roots cool to push fresh flowers through summer. Clean white lockets hover above the lacy mounded foliage from spring onward.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade with steady moisture.

directions: Grow this white-flowered form in cool, moist, well-drained soil where its low mound of gray-green foliage stays fresh all season. Keep watered in summer to sustain repeat bloom and deadhead to tidy the clump. White heart-shaped flowers appear in spring and rebloom through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade for the richest flower color.

directions: Prized for its deep crimson-red flowers, this selection wants cool, humus-rich soil and consistent moisture in dappled shade. Shear back after the first flush and keep watered to draw fresh bloom through summer. Dusky red lockets hang over the ferny foliage from spring into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool shade with good drainage.

directions: Set this compact hybrid in moist, humus-rich soil where its striking blue-gray, finely divided foliage stands out in shade. Keep evenly watered and remove spent flower stalks to prolong bloom. Deep rose-pink hearts appear in spring and continue through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in dappled shade with moist soil.

directions: Grown for its silvery blue-green mounds of ferny foliage, this selection makes a soft groundcover in cool, humus-rich soil. Give it steady moisture in shade and it will rebloom into summer. Pale pink to nearly white lockets rise above the leaves from mid-spring onward.

planting.note: Plant in spring; one of the longest-blooming and most heat-tolerant selections.

directions: This reliable hybrid tolerates more heat and sun than most and blooms for months when kept moist. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in part shade and water through dry spells to sustain flowering. Cherry-red hearts appear in mid-spring above blue-green ferny foliage and repeat until frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, part-shade beds; very long bloomer.

directions: A compact hybrid with especially long bloom, this plant forms a tidy mound of blue-gray fern-like foliage. Grow in humus-rich, evenly moist soil in part shade and keep watered for continuous flowers. Deep rose-pink hearts appear from late spring and continue through summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade with good drainage.

directions: This showy hybrid pairs bright red flowers edged in white with striking silver-blue foliage. Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in part shade and keep the roots cool for months of bloom. Glowing red-and-white hearts appear from late spring and repeat through the growing season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded bed with rich soil.

directions: Grow this white-flowered hybrid in cool, humus-rich, evenly moist soil in part shade for a long bloom season. The blue-gray ferny foliage stays neat all summer with regular water and light deadheading. Ivory-white hearts dangle above the mound from late spring into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade with steady moisture.

directions: This compact hybrid blooms heavily and long when planted in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in dappled shade. Keep the soil cool and evenly watered to maintain flowering through the season. Rosy-pink hearts appear above silvery blue-green foliage from late spring onward.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool part shade for the deepest color.

directions: Set this deep-flowered hybrid in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in part shade and keep the roots cool. Regular water and occasional deadheading extend the long bloom. Crimson to ruby-red hearts float above blue-gray ferny foliage from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded border with rich soil.

directions: Grow this vigorous selection in cool, humus-rich, evenly moist soil in part to full shade. It reblooms freely through the season if kept watered and lightly deadheaded. Large rose-pink flowers rise over the gray-green ferny mound from spring into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade for repeat bloom.

directions: This free-flowering hybrid wants fertile, moist, well-drained soil in dappled shade and cool roots. Water through summer heat and deadhead to keep the flowers coming. Deep pink-red hearts appear above the fern-like blue-green foliage from spring into fall.

planting.note: Plant after frost against a trellis; give the tendrils something to climb.

directions: Formerly listed as Dicentra scandens, this tendril-climbing relative scrambles up supports in fertile, moist, well-drained soil with part shade. Provide a trellis or shrub to climb and keep the roots cool and watered. Clusters of yellow heart-shaped flowers hang along the vine through summer and into fall, followed by ornamental seed pods.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sharply drained gritty soil; a challenging alpine.

directions: This tiny, demanding alpine needs very sharply drained gritty soil, cool conditions, and protection from wet in a trough or rock garden. Avoid rich soil and excess moisture, which rot the crown. Delicate pink lockets rise above the finely dissected silvery foliage in late spring and summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to protect the gold leaves from scorch.

directions: This selection combines golden foliage with ruby-red flowers and needs dappled shade to keep the leaves from burning. Plant in rich, moist, well-drained soil and water during dry weather. Deep rose-red hearts hang against the bright leaves in spring before the plant fades in summer heat.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed outdoors in autumn so winter cold breaks dormancy naturally.

directions: Sow seed about a quarter inch deep in fall and expect slow, uneven germination the following spring after a cold winter. Set young plants in their permanent spot early, since the deep taproot dislikes being moved, then leave the clump undisturbed to bulk up over several years. Look for spikes of star-shaped white flowers in early summer, followed by aromatic seed pods that release a citrus-scented, flammable oil. Wear gloves, as the sap can cause a sun-triggered skin rash.

planting.note: Direct sow in autumn to give seeds the chilling period they need.

directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline-to-neutral soil in full sun and choose the site carefully because this long-lived perennial resents transplanting. Water through the first season to establish the taproot, then let it fend for itself in dry spells. Rosy-pink flowers veined with darker purple open in early summer atop lemon-scented, ash-like foliage. Leave the seed heads standing for structure or cut them for arrangements.

planting.note: Autumn-sown seed germinates the next spring once cold has broken dormancy.

directions: Give this selected pure-white form a sunny, sharply drained bed and disturb the roots as little as possible when planting. It is slow to settle in but rewards patience with decades of bloom from a single crown. Clean white flowers rise on upright stems in early summer above glossy, citrus-scented leaves. Handle the plant with gloves in bright sun, since the volatile oils can irritate skin.

planting.note: Sow in fall for natural cold stratification over winter.

directions: This East Asian relative of the gas plant needs the same conditions: full sun, lean well-drained soil, and an undisturbed permanent home for its taproot. Start from fall-sown seed and be patient through slow germination and a few years to first flower. Pale pink to whitish blooms with fine purple veining appear in early summer over aromatic compound foliage. Its root bark is a traditional Chinese medicinal, but treat the plant as ornamental in the garden.

planting.note: Surface-sow in late spring to early summer for bloom the following year.

directions: Press the tiny seeds onto the soil surface and do not cover them, as they need light to sprout. Keep evenly moist until seedlings appear, then thin to give each rosette room to grow. Tall flower spikes rise the second year and open from the bottom upward in early summer; cut a spent spike back to encourage a few smaller side stems.

planting.note: Sow on the surface in early summer for pure white spires the next season.

directions: Scatter seed on firmed soil and leave it uncovered so light can trigger germination. Grow the first-year rosette in dappled shade with steady moisture. Ghostly white bells line the tall spikes in early summer of the second year; remove faded spikes before seed sets to keep the strain coming true.

planting.note: Surface-sow in summer; soft apricot bells follow the next year.

directions: Sow onto the soil surface and firm gently without burying, since the seed germinates in light. Water carefully to avoid washing seed away, and thin crowded seedlings. Warm apricot-pink flowers open along tall spikes in early summer; deadhead promptly if you want fewer stray self-sown seedlings.

planting.note: Surface-sow for dramatic white bells with maroon throats the next season.

directions: Press seed onto moist soil and leave uncovered so daylight can prompt sprouting. Give the first-year rosette rich, well-drained soil and even water. Tall white spikes marked with deep burgundy throats open in early summer of year two; stake the stems in exposed sites to keep them upright.

planting.note: Sow on the surface in summer for clean white bells the following year.

directions: Surface-sow and mist to settle the seed without covering it. Grow on in part shade with consistent moisture through the first year. Unspotted snow-white flowers crowd the spikes in early summer; cut back after flowering to tidy the plant and reduce self-seeding.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; this strain can bloom its first summer.

directions: Sow indoors on the surface of the mix under bright light about ten to twelve weeks before your last frost. Harden off and set out young plants after frost danger passes. Unlike most foxgloves, this compact strain flowers the same year, opening pastel spires in shades of pink, cream, and rose by mid to late summer.

planting.note: Surface-sow in summer; the ring of florets circles the whole stem.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the soil and keep it moist and uncovered until it sprouts. This selection holds its bells outward all around the stem rather than drooping to one side. Tall pastel spikes bloom in early summer of the second year; cut the main spike after flowering to prompt secondary stems.

planting.note: Start indoors early; blooms the first year from an early sowing.

directions: Sow on the surface indoors under lights about twelve weeks before the last frost and transplant out once settled. This day-length-neutral strain flowers its first season without needing a cold period. Soft lavender spires bloom from early summer; deadhead spent spikes for a possible rebloom.

planting.note: Start indoors early for first-year rose-pink spires.

directions: Surface-sow indoors under bright light and set plants out after frost. This selection blooms the first year regardless of day length. Rich rose-pink flowers with speckled throats line tall spikes from early summer; remove faded stems to tidy the clump and encourage side shoots.

planting.note: Start indoors early for creamy first-year bloom.

directions: Sow on the surface of a moist mix indoors under lights and grow on before transplanting after frost. The strain flowers its first summer without vernalization. Soft cream bells with maroon-freckled interiors open from early summer; cut back spent spikes to keep the display neat.

planting.note: Start indoors early for white spires the first summer.

directions: Surface-sow indoors under bright light about twelve weeks ahead of planting out. This first-year-flowering strain needs no cold spell to bloom. Clean white bells with lightly freckled throats open on tall spikes from early summer; deadhead to prompt a second flush of smaller stems.

planting.note: Start indoors early; this dwarf strain blooms its first year.

directions: Sow on the surface indoors under lights, as this compact strain needs light and warmth to sprout quickly. Transplant hardened seedlings out after the last frost. Peachy spires open early and low enough to skip staking in most gardens; deadhead to extend the show into midsummer.

planting.note: Start indoors early for first-year purple spires on a compact plant.

directions: Surface-sow indoors under bright light and warmth for fast, even germination. Set young plants out after frost has passed. Deep purple bells crowd the short, sturdy spikes the first summer, needing no staking; trim spent spikes to keep the plant blooming.

planting.note: Start indoors early for compact white spikes the first year.

directions: Sow on the surface indoors with warmth and light to speed sprouting. Grow on and transplant after the frost date. Clean white bells appear on short, self-supporting spikes the first summer; deadhead promptly to encourage continued flowering.

planting.note: Start indoors early for rose-pink spikes the first season.

directions: Surface-sow indoors under lights, keeping the mix warm and moist for quick germination. Harden off and plant out after frost. Rose-pink bells with speckled throats open early on compact, sturdy spikes; remove faded stems to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors early for pale-yellow bells with speckled throats.

directions: Sow on the surface indoors under bright light and warmth. Transplant sturdy seedlings out after frost. This dwarf strain rings its short spikes with soft primrose-yellow bells the first summer; deadhead to keep the flush going.

planting.note: Surface-sow in summer; unusual upward-facing bells follow the next year.

directions: Press seed onto moist soil and leave uncovered so light triggers germination. Grow the rosette through the first year in part shade. This selection is prized for bells that face upward, showing their freckled interiors, opening rose-pink in early summer of year two.

planting.note: Surface-sow in summer for large open bells the next year.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on firmed soil and keep it uncovered and moist. This old strain bears wider, more open bells than the species in a mix of purples, pinks, and whites. Tall spikes bloom in early summer of the second year; deadhead to slow self-seeding around the bed.

planting.note: Sow on the surface for a compact, long-lived yellow foxglove.

directions: Press seed onto moist soil and leave uncovered, keeping the surface damp until sprouts show. This dwarf perennial selection suits the front of a border or a container. Butter-yellow bells open on short spikes in early summer; trim faded stems and it often returns and reflowers reliably.

planting.note: Surface-sow this slender, long-lived species for narrow yellow bells.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep moist, since the fine seed needs light to germinate. This graceful perennial carries slim spikes densely packed with small pale-yellow bells. Flowers open in early to midsummer and the tidy clump returns for many years; leave a few seed heads if you want gentle self-sowing.

planting.note: Surface-sow this extra-tall form for statuesque copper spires.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep the seedbed moist and uncovered until sprouts appear. This robust form pushes taller spikes than the type, crowded with coppery bronze bells. Blooms open in midsummer above a basal rosette; leave the architectural dried stems for late-season structure.

planting.note: Surface-sow for spikes of small netted cream-and-bronze bells.

directions: Press seed onto moist soil and leave uncovered until it germinates. This species carries dense spikes of small cream bells intricately veined with bronze and brown. Flowers open in mid to late summer; note that all parts are highly toxic, so site it away from areas where it might be nibbled.

planting.note: Surface-sow for coffee-and-cream netted bells.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep moist until seedlings emerge. This selection bears tight spikes of small bells in creamy tan tones netted with coffee-brown veining. Flowers open in mid to late summer above a neat rosette; leave a few seed heads to self-sow, as it is short-lived.

planting.note: Surface-sow for dense spikes of tiny rusty-brown bells.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of moist soil and leave it uncovered. This distinctive species forms tight, upright spikes packed with small orange-brown bells rather than large open flowers. Blooms open in early to midsummer above glossy foliage and the tidy clump is fairly long-lived.

planting.note: Surface-sow for chocolate-brown packed spikes.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep the seedbed moist until germination. This selection is grown for its dense spikes of small bells in warm milk-chocolate brown. Flowers open in early to midsummer over glossy dark-green rosettes; a well-drained spot keeps the clump returning for several years.

planting.note: Surface-sow this shrubby species in a warm, well-drained spot.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of gritty, well-drained soil and keep barely moist. This semi-woody species has narrow evergreen leaves and prefers a hot, sharply drained site. Bells in warm orange, rust, and apricot open over a long stretch of summer; avoid heavy winter wet, which it dislikes.

planting.note: Surface-sow, or divide clumps; a true perennial in crushed-strawberry tones.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep moist, or divide established clumps in spring to keep the color true. This hybrid is a reliable perennial with large bells in a soft crushed-strawberry rose. Flowers open in early summer; divide every couple of years to keep the clump vigorous and long-lived.

planting.note: Surface-sow this compact species in a warm, well-drained spot.

directions: Press seed onto gritty, well-drained soil and leave uncovered to germinate in light. This compact species has woolly gray-green leaves and tolerates heat and lean soil. Rosy-pink bells open on short spikes in early summer; give it sharp drainage and it will often self-sow modestly.

planting.note: Set out sterile plants after frost; grown from division, not seed.

directions: This sterile hybrid does not come from seed, so start with a nursery plant and set it out after the last frost. Grow in rich, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. Vivid rose-pink bells with golden throats bloom from early summer well into fall, since the plant puts no energy into seed; cut back spent spikes to keep new ones coming.

planting.note: Set out sterile plants after frost; propagated by division only.

directions: Begin with a purchased plant, as this sterile hybrid sets no viable seed, and plant it out once frost danger has passed. Give it fertile, well-drained soil and full sun. Fiery orange-pink bells with amber throats bloom nonstop from early summer to frost; shear back finished spikes to spur repeat flowering.

planting.note: Set out sterile plants after frost; no seed is produced.

directions: This sterile cross is sold as started plants rather than seed, so plant one out after the last frost. Site it in rich, free-draining soil in full sun to part shade. Deep raspberry-pink bells with speckled cream throats bloom from early summer until frost; remove old spikes to keep the plant flowering.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost; this sterile hybrid is grown from division.

directions: Start with a nursery plant, since this sterile hybrid does not set seed, and plant it out after frost. Grow in well-drained soil in sun or light shade. Apricot-yellow bells bloom over a long season because no seed forms; shear spent spikes to prompt fresh flushes into autumn.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost; a sterile hybrid raised from division.

directions: This sterile hybrid is grown from divisions rather than seed, so plant a nursery start out after frost. Give it fertile, well-drained soil in sun to part shade. Warm spice-toned bells in shades of rust and honey bloom over a long summer season; trim finished spikes to keep it flowering.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost; this hybrid is propagated by division.

directions: This hybrid is typically grown from divisions rather than seed, so plant a nursery start out after frost. Give it well-drained soil in sun or light shade. Coppery salmon-pink bells with veined throats open on tall spikes in early summer; cut back after flowering to help the clump persist.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost; this sterile hybrid is grown from division.

directions: This sterile cross between straw and common foxglove is grown from division, so start with a nursery plant set out after frost. Give it well-drained soil in part shade to sun. Soft apricot-buff bells bloom over a long season since no seed forms; shear spent spikes to keep the flowers coming.

planting.note: Surface-sow for split, star-shaped white bells with maroon throats.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist soil and leave uncovered so light can trigger sprouting. This novelty selection has bells that split open into a flared, star-like shape. White flowers marked with deep maroon throats open on tall spikes in early summer of the second year; deadhead to reduce self-sowing.

planting.note: Surface-sow in summer for soft apricot bells the following year.

directions: Scatter seed on firmed, moist soil and leave uncovered to germinate in light. Grow the first-year rosette in dappled shade with steady water. Pale apricot bells with lightly freckled throats open on tall spikes in early summer of the second season; deadhead to slow self-seeding.

planting.note: Surface-sow this vigorous old strain for very tall mixed spires.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of moist soil and keep uncovered until it sprouts. This robust heritage strain is among the tallest foxgloves, in shades of pink, rose, and white. Big spikes bloom in early summer of the second year; stake in windy spots and leave a few seed heads for self-sowing.

planting.note: Set out divisions or pots in early spring or autumn while the soil is cool and moist.

directions: Plant with the crown level with the soil surface in humus-rich, consistently moist ground. Divide clumps every few years in early autumn to keep them vigorous, and cut back the tired foliage once plants slip into summer dormancy. Expect cheerful yellow daisies in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant this compact selection in spring for a tidy front-of-border clump.

directions: Space plants close together at the edge of a bed with the crown flush to the soil. Keep the ground evenly moist through bloom, then let the clump rest as it dies back in the heat of summer. This dwarf form covers itself in bright golden daisies in early spring.

planting.note: Set out in spring or fall in a cool, moisture-retentive spot.

directions: Plant in rich, moist soil with the crown at ground level and mulch lightly to keep roots cool. This robust, large-flowered selection makes an excellent cut flower, so pick stems as the daisies open in spring. Trim back foliage after the summer decline.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for possible first-year bloom, or set out plants in spring.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and press it into the surface, as light aids germination, at around 65 degrees. Grow on and transplant to moist, fertile soil once frost has passed. This uniform seed strain forms neat mounds topped with golden daisies in spring, sometimes flowering the first season from an early start.

planting.note: Plant in spring or autumn in dappled shade where it can naturalize.

directions: Set divisions with the crown level in cool, moist woodland soil, giving each plant room to spread by creeping roots. This vigorous species can colonize a shady bank, so lift and divide it to keep it in bounds. Small yellow daisies rise on branching stems from late spring into early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, part-shaded ground.

directions: Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil with the crown at surface level and shelter it from hot afternoon sun. This tall species bears large golden daisies on wiry stems in late spring and makes a fine cut flower. Cut plants back as the foliage yellows in summer dormancy.

planting.note: Increase this cultivar only by division, since it does not come true from seed.

directions: Plant divisions in deep, moist, humus-rich soil with the crown just at the surface. This tall, free-flowering hybrid produces bold yellow daisies excellent for cutting in mid to late spring. Divide the clumps every few years in autumn to maintain their vigor.

planting.note: Propagate this hybrid by division in spring or early autumn.

directions: Set divisions into cool, moist soil with the crown flush to the ground and mulch to conserve moisture. This heart-leaved hybrid holds its foliage longer than many kinds and carries large golden daisies in spring. Lift and split congested clumps after flowering or in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, moist, part-shaded soil.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in humus-rich ground that stays evenly moist through spring. This species has distinctive heart-shaped basal leaves and bright yellow daisies in mid spring. Divide clumps every two to three years to keep them flowering well.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in reliably moist, cool ground.

directions: Site this tall species in deep, moisture-retentive soil, ideally near a stream or in a cool woodland edge. Keep the roots damp, as it dislikes drying out, and stake the stems in exposed sites. Clusters of yellow daisies open on branching stems in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant this alpine species in spring in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Grow in a rock garden or scree bed in sharply drained but moisture-retentive soil with the crown level. This compact mountain species suits cooler climates and resents summer heat and wet, stagnant conditions. Bright yellow daisies appear on short stems from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant this alpine in spring in a cool, gritty rock garden.

directions: Set into free-draining alpine soil with the crown at the surface and top-dress with grit. This high-mountain species needs cool summers and good drainage to thrive and dislikes lowland heat. Solitary yellow daisies open on slender, hairy stems in early summer.

planting.note: Increase this double-flowered form by division, as it will not come true from seed.

directions: Plant divisions in cool, moist, fertile soil with the crown flush to the ground. This selection, also sold as Spring Beauty, bears fully double golden daisies that resemble small pompons in spring. Divide clumps in autumn every few years to keep the display strong.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, part-shaded soil for a fine-petalled spring show.

directions: Set plants with the crown at soil level in rich, evenly moist ground and mulch to keep roots cool. This selection carries starry yellow daisies with slender ray petals on tidy clumps in spring. Cut back and let it rest once the foliage fades in summer.

planting.note: Direct-sow in fall so winter cold breaks dormancy naturally.

directions: Sow seed shallowly into lean, well-drained soil and let a cold winter satisfy the stratification requirement. This prairie native forms a deep taproot, so keep it in a permanent spot and water sparingly once established. Slender pink rays droop from the central cone in early summer, usually in the second season.

planting.note: Start indoors after stratifying, then transplant once frost has passed.

directions: Start stratified seed indoors and set young plants out in full sun with good drainage. This Perennial Plant of the Year selection holds its rosy-purple petals flatter and more horizontally than the species. Enjoy the large, uniform flowers from midsummer to frost and leave seed heads for goldfinches.

planting.note: Start seed indoors after stratification and plant out past frost.

directions: Raise from chilled seed indoors and transplant into full sun with room to breathe. Known also as Ruby Star, it carries deep carmine-red petals held nearly horizontal on strong stems. The vivid blooms draw butterflies from midsummer onward and stand well without staking.

planting.note: Set out nursery divisions in spring once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant divisions or potted stock at the same depth they grew, firming soil around the crown. This compact cultivar is prized for its unusual soft-green petals and pleasant fragrance. Green blooms appear from early to late summer and hold their color best with some afternoon shade in hot climates.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow seed indoors and grow on to transplant into a sunny bed; this seed strain blooms reliably the first year. Its compact, branching habit needs no deadheading to keep flowering. Rich magenta-pink blooms cover the plant from early summer to frost, ideal for the front of a border.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set out after frost for first-year bloom.

directions: Start seed indoors under warmth and transplant to full sun after frost; this AAS-winning mix flowers the first season. A single sowing yields a blend of red, orange, gold, purple, and cream flowers. Expect a long show from early summer to fall on tidy, drought-tolerant plants.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors and plant out in full sun; this early, seed-grown strain often blooms in its first year. Its bushy, well-branched habit produces many large rose-pink flowers. Blooming starts earlier than many coneflowers and continues through summer with light deadheading.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the ground warms.

directions: Set out potted plants or divisions at their original depth and water in well. This dwarf selection stays knee-high, making it a good fit for small beds and containers. Rosy-pink petals droop gently from a coppery cone from early summer to early fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions or pots in spring once soil is workable.

directions: Plant nursery divisions in full sun with sharp drainage and keep the crown at soil level. Dark, near-black stems make a striking contrast against the bright magenta-pink flowers. Blooms hold well for cutting and keep coming from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring after the last hard freeze.

directions: Plant potted stock in full sun with excellent drainage, as double coneflowers dislike wet winter soil. This variety forms a fiery orange-red pompom center ringed by drooping orange petals. Expect showy double blooms from midsummer to fall; propagate only by division to keep it true.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring once frost has passed.

directions: Set plants in full sun with free-draining soil and avoid mulching over the crown. This compact, sturdy-stemmed selection carries bright scarlet-red flowers that hold their color well. Blooms appear the first year from spring plantings and repeat from summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring as growth resumes.

directions: Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and give the tall stems room to stand upright. Petals open green and gradually flush pink-purple toward the center as they age. The two-tone flowers make an unusual cut stem and bloom from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Plant potted stock in full sun and keep soil moist while it establishes. This compact double bears a fluffy pink pompom atop a collar of pink petals. Deadhead spent flowers to prompt fresh buds from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once the ground is workable.

directions: Set plants in full sun with sharp drainage to carry the crowns safely through winter. Large single flowers open a deep tomato-red on strong, well-branched stems. The big blooms hold their color for weeks and draw butterflies from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring after frost danger.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage and space for airflow around the stems. This was among the first fully double coneflowers, topped by a rose-pink pompom over a skirt of petals. Enjoy the unusual double blooms from midsummer into fall and divide clumps to propagate.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring when the soil warms.

directions: Plant in full sun with free-draining soil and avoid overwatering established clumps. This sturdy, compact selection carries clear lemon-yellow petals around a brown cone. Blooms hold up well and repeat from early summer through fall on strong stems.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Set potted plants in full sun with excellent drainage and keep mulch off the crown. This double coneflower forms a rounded orange pompom center over drooping orange petals. Expect richly colored double flowers from midsummer to fall; increase only by division.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once frost has passed.

directions: Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and give each clump room for air movement. This compact double produces a plush cranberry-red pompom on sturdy, branching stems. The dense double blooms last well and flower from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Set plants in full sun with well-drained soil and keep them on the dry side once rooted. This sturdy, compact selection holds vivid coral-orange single flowers on strong stems. Blooms resist fading and repeat from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring as new growth begins.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage and support for the upright stems if needed. This white selection bred by Piet Oudolf holds its petals crisply horizontal around a green-gold cone. Pure white flowers appear from midsummer to fall and look striking in a naturalistic border.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the ground is workable.

directions: Set out potted stock in full sun with free-draining soil and water while establishing. This selection opens golden-yellow flowers with a honey fragrance and a darker central cone. Bright, sweetly scented blooms carry from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and give the tall stems room to stand. This vigorous double bears a creamy-white pompom over a skirt of white petals. The large double flowers are lightly fragrant and bloom from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once soil warms.

directions: Set plants in full sun with sharp drainage and keep the crown free of heavy mulch. This compact selection carries broad, bright yellow petals around a golden cone. Cheerful flowers cover the tidy mound from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant potted stock in full sun with good drainage and avoid soggy winter soil. This compact double forms a deep red pompom center ringed by red-purple petals. The rich double blooms hold their color and flower from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Set plants in full sun with sharp drainage and keep them lean rather than richly fed. This compact, well-branched selection carries warm adobe-orange single flowers. The sturdy stems resist flopping and bloom heavily from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost passes.

directions: Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and give the crown room to stay dry. This double bears a rounded pompom in soft guava-pink tones over matching petals. The fluffy double flowers last well and bloom from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once the ground is workable.

directions: Set plants in full sun with good drainage and space them for airflow. This vigorous selection opens large soft-yellow flowers that fade to cream, with a light fragrance. The broad blooms held on strong stems make good cut flowers from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant potted stock in full sun with free-draining soil and water while it settles in. This selection bears golden-yellow petals around a rich orange cone and carries a light scent. Warm, glowing flowers appear from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Set plants in full sun with average, well-drained soil and space for airflow. This selection bears clean white petals around a green-tinged cone that ages to bronze. Reliable white flowers appear from midsummer to fall and are good for cutting.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once soil warms.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage; this compact, floriferous selection needs little fuss. Its narrow, quilled pink petals give the flowers an airy, delicate look above a coppery cone. Long-blooming from early summer to fall, it is a strong pollinator draw.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Set plants in full sun with sharp drainage and keep them on the leaner side. This compact, well-branched selection opens coral-pink flowers that hold their color well. Bright single blooms appear from early summer into fall on tidy plants.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage and allow room for the tall, branching stems. Two-tone flowers open with orange petals shading to gold at the tips around a dark cone. The many bicolor blooms carry from midsummer into fall and draw butterflies.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Set plants in full sun with free-draining soil and give room for airflow. Flowers open orange and mature through coral to pink, so several shades show at once. The color-shifting single blooms carry from midsummer into fall on strong stems.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once soil is workable.

directions: Plant in full sun with sharp drainage; this dwarf selection suits containers and the front of a border. Its compact mounds carry bright rose-pink flowers well above the foliage. Free-flowering from early summer to fall with no need to deadhead.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost passes.

directions: Set plants in full sun with well-drained soil and keep them lean and on the dry side. This compact, sturdy selection carries deep burgundy-red single flowers on strong stems. The rich blooms resist fading and repeat from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage and allow room for the large, robust clumps. Broad, overlapping pink petals give the big flowers a full, rounded look on tall stems. The oversized blooms carry from midsummer into fall and make excellent cut flowers.

planting.note: Sow outdoors around the last spring frost or start indoors a few weeks earlier.

directions: Sow seed about one-quarter inch deep in lean, well-drained soil and thin seedlings to stand well apart. Give the plants full sun and avoid rich or soggy ground, as the taproot resents wet feet. Steel-blue spherical heads open in mid to late summer above deeply cut gray-green foliage; cut a few for drying just before the tiny florets fully open.

planting.note: Direct sow near the last frost date once the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant in full sun in average, sharply drained soil and space generously to allow air movement. This tall species may need a discreet stake in exposed sites but generally stands on its own sturdy stems. Grayish-blue globes crowd the branching tops in summer and draw bees and butterflies; leave some seed heads for goldfinches in autumn.

planting.note: Sow in place in spring; this vigorous species self-sows readily.

directions: Give this large species room at the back of a border in full sun and poor to average soil. Deadhead spent heads to curb prolific self-seeding, which can become weedy in favorable spots. Pale grayish-white to silvery-blue drumstick heads rise on tall branching stems in mid to late summer and buzz with pollinators.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in a sunny spot with room to spread.

directions: Site this stately, tall species where its height suits the back of a bed and give it full sun and lean, free-draining ground. It tolerates drought once established and rarely needs feeding. Grayish-white spherical heads top the branched stems in summer and are excellent for cutting and drying.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost in warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Grow in full sun and sharply drained, even gravelly soil where its silvery, white-woolly stems show best. Cut plants back after flowering to tidy them and sometimes encourage a lighter rebloom. Intensely blue globe heads appear in mid to late summer over finely divided, spiny foliage.

planting.note: Sow in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant in full sun in gritty, well-drained soil and avoid overwatering. The deeply lobed, spiny leaves are green above with a pale, felted underside. Pale blue to grayish spherical heads open in summer and attract bees; the seed heads dry well for arrangements.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in spring; divide clumps every few years in spring or fall.

directions: Plant divisions in full sun and lean, well-drained soil, keeping the crown at ground level. This selection is prized for its deep steel-blue heads and a tendency to rebloom if the first flush is sheared off. Expect richly colored globes over gray-green foliage from midsummer onward; leave some for drying.

planting.note: Plant out in spring; propagate true to type by division or root cuttings.

directions: Give this tall classic full sun and average, sharply drained soil, with space for its branching stems. It seldom needs staking but appreciates shelter from strong wind. Large steel-blue spherical heads open in mid to late summer and are a magnet for bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost or set out plants in spring.

directions: Plant in full sun in ordinary, well-drained soil and thin or space so air moves freely around the clumps. Drought tolerant once rooted, it needs little care beyond deadheading. Bright blue drumstick heads appear across summer above coarse, thistle-like foliage and are loved by pollinators.

planting.note: Set out in spring; divide established clumps to keep them vigorous.

directions: Grow in full sun and lean, free-draining soil, avoiding rich beds that soften the stems. This selection carries luminous mid-blue heads on strong branching stems that rarely flop. Flowering peaks in mid to late summer and the round heads dry beautifully for indoor use.

planting.note: Plant in spring; propagate by division to keep the silver stems true.

directions: Set in full sun and gritty, sharply drained soil where the pale, silvery white stems contrast with the blue heads. It is very drought tolerant and dislikes winter wet, so avoid heavy, waterlogged ground. Steely blue globes open in summer atop striking platinum-toned stems and finely cut foliage.

planting.note: Plant out in spring; divide in spring or autumn to maintain the clone.

directions: Grow in full sun and average, well-drained soil where the striking red stems can be admired. It is more compact and better behaved than the plain species but still appreciates space. White spherical heads on deep red stems open in mid to late summer and dry well for arrangements.

planting.note: Set out in spring; divide clumps every few years to keep them tidy.

directions: Plant in full sun and free-draining soil, giving the clump room to develop its branching stems. Drought tolerant and undemanding, it needs only occasional deadheading and a spring cutback. Crisp white globe heads open through summer above gray-green foliage and are excellent for cutting fresh or dried.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring while the clump is dormant or just breaking bud.

directions: Tuck divisions into cool woodland soil with the growing points at the surface and firm gently. Keep the ground steadily moist the first year and top-dress yearly with leaf mold. Cut back tattered leaves in late winter to reveal the violet-purple spring blooms held on wiry stems.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early autumn in dappled shade.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in a rich, moisture-retentive bed under high shade. Water regularly until established and mulch each autumn with shredded leaves. Trim away worn foliage before growth resumes so the large rose-pink, white-spurred flowers show off in mid-spring.

planting.note: Lift and divide in early spring, replanting immediately in shade.

directions: Plant crowns shallowly in cool, organic soil where they are sheltered from hot afternoon sun. Keep evenly moist and feed with an annual leaf-mold mulch. Remove old leaves in late winter to display the pure white spurred flowers that open in early to mid-spring.

planting.note: Best planted in spring in a cool, shaded rock or woodland pocket.

directions: Site this miniature form in gritty, humus-rich soil with reliable shade and even moisture. Because it stays small, give it a spot at the front of a bed or in a trough where its scale is appreciated. Clip spent leaves in late winter so the white-and-lilac spring flowers stand clear of the low mound.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into rich, shaded soil.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in cool, leafy ground and water through the establishment year. Mulch annually to conserve moisture and protect shallow rhizomes. Shear old foliage in late winter to show the long-spurred purple flowers that appear in spring.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring or early fall in partial to full shade.

directions: Plant crowns shallowly in humus-rich soil beneath trees or shrubs, spacing them to knit into a groundcover. This tough hybrid tolerates dry shade once established but looks best with steady moisture. Cut back last year's leaves in late winter to feature the crimson-and-yellow spring flowers and the red-flushed new foliage.

planting.note: Set divisions in spring or fall; excellent for dry shade under trees.

directions: Plant in leafy, well-drained soil where it can spread slowly into a dense mat, even under the drip line of established trees. Water the first season to establish; afterward it endures periods of dry shade. Shear old leaves in late winter to reveal the soft yellow flowers and bronze-tinted spring growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall as a semi-evergreen groundcover in shade.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil in rich, shaded ground and space to form a weed-suppressing carpet. The glossy leaves are marbled red on emergence and stay handsome much of the year. Mow or shear the foliage down in late winter to spotlight the bright yellow spring flowers and clean new growth.

planting.note: Divide in spring; a compact form for cool, shaded pockets.

directions: Plant this dainty deciduous hybrid in cool, humus-rich soil in dappled shade. Keep it evenly moist and mulch with leaf mold to mimic its woodland home. Trim old foliage before growth begins so the small pure-white flowers show against bronze-flushed spring leaves.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a shaded, moisture-retentive bed.

directions: Set crowns shallowly in cool, leafy soil under high shade and water through the first summer. This compact form knits slowly into a neat clump. Cut away tired leaves in late winter to reveal the lilac-pink flowers dangling above fresh spring growth.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall as an evergreen shade groundcover.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil in rich, shaded ground and let them spread into a glossy evergreen carpet. The leaves emerge coppery and hold through much of the year. Cut back old foliage in late winter to feature the yellow spring blooms and fresh growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in partial shade for its unusual orange flowers.

directions: Plant crowns shallowly in humus-rich, well-drained soil in dappled shade where the coppery-orange flowers stand out. Water through establishment and mulch with leaf mold. Shear old leaves in late winter so the distinctive spring blooms and red-tinted new foliage show clearly.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in dappled shade with steady moisture.

directions: Set the crown at the soil surface in cool, organic soil under high shade and keep it evenly moist. This selection offers especially warm orange spring flowers over red-flushed leaves. Remove the previous year's foliage in late winter so the blooms are not hidden.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; spreads to form a deciduous groundcover.

directions: Plant crowns in cool, humus-rich soil in shade where the running rhizomes can knit into a mat. Water the first year and mulch to keep roots cool. Cut old leaves back in late winter so the small red-and-yellow spring flowers appear over fresh growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall as a semi-evergreen for shaded borders.

directions: Set crowns in rich, well-drained soil in partial shade and water until established. This species carries airy sprays of many small cream-and-pink flowers. Shear old foliage in late winter so the delicate spring blooms rise clearly above the clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a cool, shaded rock or woodland bed.

directions: Plant this compact Japanese species in gritty, humus-rich soil with even moisture and reliable shade. Its small size suits the front of a bed or a shaded trough. Trim old leaves in late winter to reveal the spurless white bell-shaped flowers that open in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade; foliage often persists through winter.

directions: Set crowns in cool, leaf-rich soil in dappled shade with steady moisture. New leaves often emerge richly tinted and may hold their color into winter. Remove any tattered foliage in late winter so the white to lilac spring flowers stand out.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, shaded spot with rich soil.

directions: Plant this Chinese species in humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade sheltered from drying wind. It bears comparatively large, long-spurred flowers over arrow-shaped, often mottled leaves. Cut old foliage in late winter and mulch to protect the crown, then enjoy the spring bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, shaded, well-drained soil.

directions: Set crowns in cool, organic soil in dappled shade and keep evenly moist. This evergreen Chinese species carries clear yellow, long-spurred flowers over a notably long spring season. Trim any worn leaves in late winter to keep the flowers well displayed.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, shaded bed; evergreen in mild areas.

directions: Plant this bold evergreen species in rich, well-drained soil in partial shade with protection from hard freezes. Its long, spiny-edged leaves and tall sprays of spidery flowers make a striking clump. Remove winter-worn leaves before the spring flush and mulch the crown in colder zones.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, shaded spot with rich, draining soil.

directions: Set the crown in humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade sheltered from cold wind. This selection is prized for warm caramel-yellow flowers held on tall, arching sprays. Cut back winter-damaged foliage in late winter and mulch the crown where hard frosts occur.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a protected, shaded bed with good drainage.

directions: Plant in rich, free-draining soil in partial shade where the dramatic spiny-toothed leaves can be admired. New growth emerges heavily mottled before maturing, and tall sprays of pale flowers rise in spring. Shear tired foliage in late winter and mulch the crown in colder gardens.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, shaded, well-drained soil.

directions: Set crowns in cool, humus-rich soil in dappled shade and keep steadily moist. This species bears distinctive, strongly reflexed white-and-purple flowers that look almost like tiny shooting stars. Remove old leaves in late winter so the unusual spring blooms show clearly.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded bed with rich, draining soil.

directions: Plant this evergreen Chinese species in humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade. It produces abundant sprays of tiny white star-shaped flowers over spiny, often mottled leaves. Trim winter-worn foliage in late winter to feature the airy spring bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, shaded spot with rich soil.

directions: Set the crown in cool, organic, well-drained soil in partial shade sheltered from drying wind. This evergreen species carries large pale-yellow, long-spurred flowers over handsome mottled new leaves. Cut back old foliage in late winter and mulch the crown to protect it.

planting.note: Plant in spring; running rhizomes form a spreading groundcover.

directions: Plant in cool, humus-rich soil in partial shade where the running rhizomes can spread into a loose colony. It bears comparatively large rose-pink, long-spurred flowers for its modest height. Shear old leaves in late winter so the spring blooms rise clearly above the fresh foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, shaded soil; long spring bloom season.

directions: Set the crown in humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade and keep evenly moist. This vigorous hybrid sends up repeated wands of amber-yellow, long-spurred flowers over a long spring season. Remove winter-worn leaves before growth begins and mulch to conserve moisture.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded bed with rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in cool, humus-rich soil in dappled shade and water through establishment. This tall hybrid bears many white flowers flushed and spurred in raspberry pink over strikingly mottled new leaves. Cut back old foliage in late winter to display the spring bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, shaded spot with rich soil.

directions: Set crowns in humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade sheltered from cold wind. This selection is grown as much for its long, dramatically spiny-edged leaves as for its pale spidery flowers. Remove winter-worn foliage in late winter and mulch the crown in colder gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, shaded soil.

directions: Plant crowns shallowly in cool, leaf-rich soil in dappled shade and keep evenly moist. This compact selection offers rich mauve-purple flowers over red-mottled spring foliage. Shear old leaves in late winter so the blooms show cleanly above fresh growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded bed with rich, draining soil.

directions: Set the crown in humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade and keep moist through the first summer. This hybrid produces crisp white flowers with contrasting purple centers over mottled new leaves. Trim old foliage in late winter to feature the spring bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; foliage darkens in winter cold.

directions: Plant crowns in rich, well-drained soil in partial shade as an evergreen groundcover. This selection is notable for winter leaves that flush to deep maroon and near-black in cold weather. Shear the old foliage in late winter to reveal the yellow spring flowers and fresh green growth.

planting.note: Plant out young plants after the last frost into gritty, free-draining soil.

directions: Set plants at the same depth they grew in their pots and firm the soil around a spreading crown. This selection produces an especially long, dense flush of two-toned daisies that shift from white to rose. Cut back hard in late winter to keep the mat tidy and encourage fresh growth.

planting.note: Plant after frost danger passes in a sunny, sharply drained spot.

directions: Plant into lean, gritty soil where the compact habit can knit into a low carpet. It flowers continuously from late spring to frost with little care beyond an occasional trim. Deadheading is unnecessary, but a hard cutback in late winter renews the clump.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or plant divisions in spring.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a fast-draining mix and keep just barely moist until sprouts appear. This coastal native tolerates salt spray, sandy soil, and wind, making it ideal for seaside and rock gardens. Lavender daisies with yellow centers open in late spring; shear spent flowers to prompt a second flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring into gritty soil with excellent drainage.

directions: Set the fleshy-leaved rosette level with the soil surface and avoid mulch that traps moisture on the crown. Larger pink daisies stand above the gray-green foliage over a long summer season. Remove faded blooms regularly to keep flowers coming into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and sharp drainage.

directions: Plant this dwarf coastal selection into lean, sandy soil where it forms a tight low cushion. Pink-lavender daisies cover the mounded foliage in late spring and repeat through summer. It thrives on neglect once rooted and resents rich, wet ground.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for bloom the following summer.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it cool and moist while it germinates. This western native forms upright clumps topped with lavender-blue rayed daisies in early to midsummer. Cut stems back after the first flush to tidy the plant and encourage rebloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and average, well-drained soil.

directions: Set plants at their original depth and space them to allow good air movement. Semi-double lavender-blue daisies with bright yellow eyes appear over several weeks in early summer. Shear the clump after blooming and it often flowers again in late summer.
